coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots embedded in silanized cellulose nanofibers: A ensitive and ustainable platform for uranium detection and remediation

coredata.dc:description: The rise of uranium ions in groundwater in Northern India has raised serious health concerns, including increased cancer cases. This study introduces a novel solution using a hydrothermal method to synthesize highly fluorescent and stable carbon dots (CDs) from Kalanchoe pinnata. These CDs were embedded in silanized cellulose nanofibers (SCNFs) to create an eco-friendly dual-functional adsorbent and fluorescent probe (SCNFs@CDs) for the remediation of UO2 2+ ions. Characterization confirmed the successful integration of CDs, preserving the nanofibrous structure necessary for stability and recovery. SCNFs@CDs demonstrated a strong affinity for UO2 2+ ions, even amid competing ions, evidenced by a linear decline in the fluorescence intensity at 360 nm when increasing the concentration, achieving a low limit of detection of 1.64 nM. Adsorption investigation demonstrated pseudo-second order with a maximal adsorption capacity of 196.07 mg g−1, signifying chemisorption. Remarkably, SCNFs@CDs maintained 87 % adsorption efficiency after five cycles of use. Our findings underscore the potential of SCNFs@CDs as an effective, and sustainable solution which can be used both for UO2 2+ ions sensing and adsorption from aqueous media, contributing to eco-friendly water purification technologies.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots embedded in silanized cellulose nanofibers: A ensitive and ustainable platform for uranium detection and remediation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rise of uranium ions in groundwater in Northern India has raised serious health concerns, including increased cancer cases. This study introduces a novel solution using a hydrothermal method to synthesize highly fluorescent and stable carbon dots (CDs) from Kalanchoe pinnata. These CDs were embedded in silanized cellulose nanofibers (SCNFs) to create an eco-friendly dual-functional adsorbent and fluorescent probe (SCNFs@CDs) for the remediation of UO2 2+ ions. Characterization confirmed the successful integration of CDs, preserving the nanofibrous structure necessary for stability and recovery. SCNFs@CDs demonstrated a strong affinity for UO2 2+ ions, even amid competing ions, evidenced by a linear decline in the fluorescence intensity at 360 nm when increasing the concentration, achieving a low limit of detection of 1.64 nM. Adsorption investigation demonstrated pseudo-second order with a maximal adsorption capacity of 196.07 mg g−1, signifying chemisorption. Remarkably, SCNFs@CDs maintained 87 % adsorption efficiency after five cycles of use. Our findings underscore the potential of SCNFs@CDs as an effective, and sustainable solution which can be used both for UO2 2+ ions sensing and adsorption from aqueous media, contributing to eco-friendly water purification technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nuclear energy has recently received enormous focus as a viable alternative to traditional fossil fuels, with uranium playing a critical role in its production. Though, uranium containing wastewater from mining, industrial process, nuclear reactors, and natural sources poses major environmental and human health risks due to extreme toxicity and persistence of uranyl ions (UO2 2+) (Coyte et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2024). The long-term effects on ecosystems and human health can arise from their ability to permeate soil, pollute groundwater, and infiltrate the food chain (Hogan, 2016). The World Health Organisation (WHO) established an acceptable level of 30 ppb for uranium contamination in drinking water owing to its significant solubility and mobility in aqueous environments (Sahoo et al., 2020). Consequently, developing sensitive, rapid detection and efficient removal methods for UO2 2+ ions is of paramount importance. Several methods for the detection of uranium have been developed to date, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Maheswari et al., 2007), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) (Jamali et al., 2006), atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Ruan et al., 2007), and fluorescence sensing (Wang et al., 2022). However, complex sample preparation, labor intensive procedures, high operational costs, low sensitivity, and the need for special substates limit the application of these methods. Amongst all, fluorescence (FL) analysis has become more prevalent for the determination of UO2 2+ ions because of its ease of sample preparation, rapidity, and sensitivity (Wang et al., 2022). For the removal of uranium on a large-scale, methods such as solvent extraction (Rao et al., 2024), membrane dialysis (Yang et al., 2020), adsorption (Lucaci and Bulgariu, 2024), and chemical precipitation (Aslani and Amik, 2021; Wang et al., 2020b) are commonly applied. However, the high cost and generation of secondary pollution associated with these methods remain major concerns. Among these, adsorption is frequently recognized as the most advantageous approach due to its versatility, cost effectiveness, minimal secondary pollution, and ability to remove even trace levels of potentially hazardous metal ions (Humelnicu et al., 2010).

Various advanced materials, including semiconductor-type quantum dots (Dutta and Kumar, 2016), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) (Gumber et al., 2022; Lei et al., 2021), fluorescent polymers, luminescent europium complexes, and DNAzyme-based sensors (Bai et al., 2022), have been explored in the realm of UO2 2+ ion detection and adsorption. However, most of these materials are utilized for either the detection or removal, with limited reports on integrated dual-function materials (Liu et al., 2022; Qin et al., 2020). Carbon dots (CDs), a novel type of carbon based nanomaterial, have garnered significant interest as fluorescent sensors owing to high optical absorptivity, strong fluorescence properties, chemical stability, low toxicity, and the presence of a diverse range of functional groups on their surface (Alafeef et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024a). Consequently, fluorescent CDs have been extensively used in heavy metal ions detection (Munusamy et al., 2023; Nagaraj et al., 2022; Samota et al., 2022); however, their potential for UO2 2+ ions detection has remained relatively less explored (Sun et al., 2023). In most of the reported studies (Shamsipur et al., 2018; Soni et al., 2024), a one-pot reaction of precursors with specified functional groups leads to the functionalization of CDs. Functionalized CD synthesis often involves high heating temperatures, leading to unwanted carbonization and degradation or polymerization of the functional molecules, consequently defying the purpose of functionalization (Manikandan and Lee, 2022). In the present study to addresses this challenge, we propose a novel post-modification strategy incorporating highly fluorescent CDs into a silanized cellulose nanofiber (SCNFs) network to develop a dual-functional material (SCNFs@CDs) for the sensing and removal of hazardous UO2 2+ ion. Cellulose nanofibers (CNFs) are the most robust support for the immobilization of CDs owing to their unique traits, including biodegradability, exceptional specific strength, a high surface-to-volume ratio, favorable light transmittance, a low thermal expansion coefficient, and an abundance of surface-active hydroxyl groups that facilitate further modifications. These characteristics make CNFs a superior matrix for sensor design and adsorption applications (Khan et al., 2024). Additionally, CNFs porous structure enhances sensor sensitivity, analyte diffusion (Mohammadnia et al., 2022). In recent years, CNFs/CDs composite materials have been investigated for either detection or adsorption of heavy metal ions (Song et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2016, 2023); however, their potential for dual-function UO2 2+ ion sensing and adsorption remains largely unexplored.

The key innovation of this work is the use of a novel carbon precursor, Kalanchoe pinnata leaves for CDs synthesis. Leaves of Kalanchoe pinnata plant are reported to have high carbon content (polysaccharides, phenolic compound, flavonoids), which facilitates efficient carbonization and in situ surface passivation for CDs synthesis. Their intrinsic heteroatom content also improves the fluorescence properties of CDs. Moreover, leaves are readily available, cost-free materials that do not contain any toxic or harmful chemicals, resulting in an extremely low environmental footprint. Such properties make Kalanchoe pinnata leaves an eco-friendly, sustainable and highly effective precursor of CDs synthesis (Pattewar, 2012; Zawirska-Wojtasiak et al., 2019). The synthesized CDs were subsequently embedded into the robust network of silanized cellulose nanofibers (SCNFs). The pendant amino groups of silanized CNFs enabled strong covalent interaction with carboxylic group of CDs. The SCNFs@CDs composite not only showed strong fluorescent behavior but also endowed high adsorption capacity. Derived from Kalanchoe pinnata leaves, the CDs provide a sustainable fluorescence source, while the SCNFs’ high surface area and structural integrity facilitate the adsorption of UO2 2+ ion. This novel strategy broadens the application of natural resources derived CDs based composite material for dual sensing and removal of environmental pollutants, making it an effective tool for environmental monitoring and water purification.
The complete information on chemicals used and characterization techniques employed for describing CDs, SCNFs, SCNFs@CDs are available in the supplementary material in Sections S1 and S2.

In this research, a novel carbon precursor, leaves of Kalanchoe pinnata , was used for the synthesis of CDs via a simple hydrothermal approach, based on previously reported protocols (Bano et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020a). Briefly, 70 mL distilled water containing 2 g of dried leaves powder of Kalanchoe pinnata was given a heat treatment at 200 °C for 5 h in Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was cooled to ambient temperature to obtain a yellowish solution that was subsequently centrifuged (10000 rpm, 10 min) to eliminate the larger particles. The yellowish supernatant was then put in the dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff: 3500 Da) with distilled water and dialyzed for three days to remove tiny molecules (Ullal et al., 2024; Yuan et al., 2014). The dialyzed sample was stored in the refrigerator for further application. A segment of the dialyzed sample was freeze-dried to obtain a powder of the CDs for characterization. The schematic step for the synthesis of CDs has been shown in Scheme 1 (a). Quantum yield (QY) of synthesized CDs was evaluated using single-point comparative fluorescence method. It is a simple, accurate and widely accepted method to determine relative QY of a material by comparing fluorescence intensities with a standard under same conditions (Phull et al., 2024; Samota et al., 2022). Herein, quinine sulfate solution (QY = 0.546 in 1M M H2SO4) was used as a reference to evaluate the QY of CDs (λ 360 nm). In order to minimize the re-absorbance and inner filter effects, the absorbance intensities (λ 360 nm) were kept under 0.1OD (optical density). QY values were computed using equation (1):
(1)
Q x = Q s t d

)

)
In this instance, Q, A, η and I denote quantum yield, absorbance, refractive index (1.33 for both reference and sample), and integral area of fluorescence intensity, respectively. Here, the subscript std denotes the standard reference, whereas x signifies CDs.

Before preparation of the composite, cellulose nanofibrils (CNFs) were isolated from hemp stalks and silanized with APTES to enhance the stability, dispersibility and surface reactivity of CNFs to interact with CDs (Komal et al., 2019). APTES provide primary amine groups (-NH2) to the surface of cellulose nanofibrils. However, these amine groups cannot directly bind with -COOH functional groups of carbon dot and therefore, conventionally, crosslinkers like glutaraldehyde (GA) are utilized to bind biomolecules or other analyte to APTES functionalized surfaces. However, due to presence of two reactive aldehyde groups in GA, it may create undesirable mesh like crosslinking. Considering this, 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC)- N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) coupling agents were used. EDC can bind with the -COOH group of CDs forming an unstable o-acylisourea intermediate which in turn get stabilized by NHS by forming NHS ester, making the -COOH group highly reactive towards primary amine groups of APTES (Udomsom et al., 2021). The detailed procedure and schematics for isolation of CNFs from hemp stalk and silanization with APTES are given in Sections S3 and S4 of the supplementary file. In brief, 10 mg of silanized cellulose nanofibrils (SCNF) were dispersed into a beaker contained 10 mL of CD solution. The dispersion was magnetically stirred overnight with coupling agents (EDC and NHS) to facilitate the interaction between SCNFs and CDs. Thereafter, the mixed suspension was subsequently stored in refrigerator at −20 °C for more than 12 h to ensure complete freezing. The frozen samples were then freeze-dried, resulting in the formation of SCNFs@CDs composite (You et al., 2016). Scheme 1(b) exhibits reaction steps for the synthesis of SCNFs@CDs nanocomposite.

To check the optical activity of the composite material, first of all UV–VIS spectrum of aqueous solution of SCNFs@CDs was recorded. The material exhibited a UV–VIS peak at 320 nm as shown in Fig. 4 (a). Further, the fluorescence (FL) behavior of the SCNFs@CDs solution was tested at different excitation wavelengths and solution pH. The material showed maximum FL intensity at 360 nm and neutral solution pH. Further, the following procedure was followed in FL measurements with UO2 2+ ions on SCNFs@CDs: A quartz cuvette was first filled with 2 mL of the SCNFs@CDs solution, and then with a micropipette, different concentrations of the uranyl stock solution were injected one at a moment. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Compared to a blank solution, the analytical signal showed a decrease in fluorescence emission intensity with increase in UO2 2+ ion concentration. Further, to validate the proposed methodology, the presence of uranium in lake water samples (pH 7.8, conductivity: 530 μS/cm) was examined. Prior to the experiment, lake water was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to eliminate solid suspensions and other contaminants and pH was adjusted to 7. The filtered water samples were spiked with various concentrations of UO2 2+ (0.5 μM, 10 μM, 40 μM), and the unknown concentrations were calculated to find the percentage recovery of spiked samples.

The UO2 2+ ions adsorption ability of SCNFs@CDs was evaluated in an aqueous solution. For adsorption studies, SCNFs@CDs powder was added to 100 ml of UO2 2+ solutions with different concentrations of UO2 2+ ions, while varying the test parameters including solution pH (pH 2 to pH 8), contact time (0–35 min), initial concentration of UO2 2+ ions in the solution (50–300 mg L−1), and the adsorbent dosage (20–100 mg/100 mL). The solution was stirred until equilibrium followed by filtration with a 0.45 μm syringe filter. The concentration of UO2 2+ ions in the sample before and after adsorption was measured using UV–VIS spectroscopy. Direct quantification of UO2 2+ ions was challenging due to its weak absorption peak at 370 nm in the UV spectrum. To overcome this, complex of UO2 2+ ions with Arsenazo (III) was prepared, that absorbs at 651 nm (Kaur et al., 2021). Adsorption capacity and removal percentage of the composite material were calculated using equations SI and S2 given in supplementary file. For the pH investigations, different pH solutions were formulated using 0.1 M HCl/NaOH. In addition, surface charge of SCNFs@CDs was determined using the zero-point charge (ZPC) method to ascertain the optimum pH for the adsorption of UO2 2+ ions. Further, to investigate the possible interactions between SCNFs@CDs and UO2 2+ ions and the adsorption mechanism of UO2 2+ ions, experimental results were investigated using the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models, along with pseudo-first order (PFO) and pseudo-second order (PSO) kinetic models. Table S1 (supplementary data) shows the equations used for fitting these models. Further, the reusability and desorption behavior of SCNFs@CDs was also studied. The adsorbed UO2 2+ ions were removed by treating the saturated adsorbents by 100 ml of 0.1N HCl solution for a duration of 4 h, followed by centrifugation to isolate the regenerated SCNFs@CDs. The composite was further reused for 5 cycles to evaluate its adsorption capacity and regeneration ability.

Through FTIR analysis, presence of different functional groups on CDs, CNFs, SCNFs, and SCNFs@CDs were examined. Fig. 1 (a) shows the FTIR spectra for CDs, CNFs, SCNFs, and SCNFs@CDs. The IR peak at 3432 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of CDs correspond to the stretching vibration associated with O-H group. The peaks identified at 1380 cm−1, 1639 cm−1, and 2910 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of the C-N, C=O, and C-H functional groups, respectively. Peaks in the 1050-1170 cm−1 region correspond to C-H, and C-O stretching vibrations (Li et al., 2019). The FTIR spectrum of raw material (Kalanchoe pinnata) is provided in the supplementary file (Section S5 Fig. S1). Besides, FTIR spectra of CNFs, SCNFs, and SCNFs@CDs were characterized by a significant presence of hydroxyl groups, exhibiting a wide peak typically observed from 3480 to 3200 cm−1. Peaks corresponding to β-glycosidic vibrations, pyranose ring structure vibrations, and asymmetric C-O-C vibrations were witnessed at 1031 cm−1, and 897 cm−1 respectively (Kaushik and Singh, 2011). On comparing the FTIR spectra of CNFs and SCNFs, additional peaks appear in SCNFs at 1640 cm−1, 1460 cm−1, 1300 cm−1, 1165 cm−1 and which can be attributed to amino groups, Si-C, Si-O, respectively (Hokkanen et al., 2014). In the FTIR spectrum of SCNFs@CDs, an extra characteristic peak can be observed at 1560 cm−1 that may correspond to N-H bending, suggesting SCNFs have reacted to CDs. To summarize, it is demonstrated that the amino groups of SCNFs reacted to the C=O of the CDs, which lead to the formation of -CONH-.

Fig. 1 (b) depicts the XRD profile of the CDs, CNFs, SCNFs, and SCNFs@CDs. The XRD patterns of CDs demonstrated a wider peak at 2θ = 24.07° and a smaller peak at 2θ = 42.77°, which corresponds to the (002) and (100) lattice planes, respectively in carbon-based materials. The broad peak indicates the existence of disordered carbon atoms inside the structure. The adequate level of graphitization observed is ascribed to the incorporation of nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional sites onto the surface of CDs, that facilitates the development of graphitic domains within the structure. The small peak at 2θ = 42.77° suggests partial graphitization of the CDs. The estimated interlayer spacing of the synthesized CDs is 0.38 nm and 0.21 nm, as determined through the (002) and (100) lattice planes, respectively. The interlayer distance between the (002) lattice plane compared to that of bulk graphite (0.34 nm) is greater, owing to the abundance of functional groups, especially nitrogen and oxygen, clustered right next to the CDs edges. These two diffraction peaks at 24.07° and 42.77° are signatures of carbon nanostructures, indicating closely packed carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen atoms (Atchudan et al., 2018; Ferjani et al., 2024).

Hemp stalk-derived cellulose nanofibers (CNFs) are characterized by crystalline peaks at 15.52°, 16.4°, 22.37°, and 34.23°, corresponding to the (110), (10‾1), (002), and (040) reflections, respectively. The peak at 2θ = 22.37°, a characteristic peak of CNFs, is associated with carbon, as identified in JCPDS card no. 50–0926 (Kaur et al., 2023). After silanization with APTES, the XRD pattern of the SCNFs shows significant deviation from the pristine CNF pattern. Notably, the primary peak at 2θ = 22.37° splits into peaks at 22.5° and 23.8°, indicating modification in the crystallographic structure of cellulose. Additionally, shifts in peaks from 15.52° to 15.7°, along with a change in intensity and appearance of a new peak at 28.02°, suggest alterations in the crystalline structure of CNFs due to the incorporation of APTES, confirming the successful functionalization of CNFs with APTES (Komal et al., 2019). The XRD spectra of SCNF and SCNFs@CDs were compared to study the effect of CD inclusion on SCNF crystal structure. The XRD spectra of SCNFs@CDs showed significant changes compared to that of SCNF (Fig. 1 (b)). Specifically, the peak at 15.7° disappeared, and the peaks at 22.5°, 23.8° and 28.02° shifted to 22.71° and 23.7°, 28.12°, respectively. These changes indicated that the CDs were successfully incorporated into the SCNF matrix, leading to the disruption or amorphization of specific crystalline regions. This analysis confirms the successful embedding of CDs within the SCNF matrix. Next, the Raman spectra of synthesized CDs was recorded to confirm graphitic carbon. The D band and G band, which indicate amorphous and graphitic carbon in Raman spectra, appear weak (Fig. S2) owing to the strong fluorescence interference of CDs. Similar instances of fluorescence interference in Raman spectra have been reported in previous studies (Alarfaj et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2020).

The TEM and HRTEM (Fig. 1(c)) analysis showed the size, morphology, and atomic lattice fringes of the synthesized carbon dots. The TEM results (Fig. 1 (c(i)) demonstrated that the CD particles were spherical, uniformly distributed, and free of aggregation. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1c(ii)) shows high and low graphitization levels in the CDs' inner core and outer shell, respectively. The size distribution histogram (Fig. 1 (c(ii)(inset)) indicates a narrow distribution of CDs with an average size of particle ≈3.5 nm. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern observed lattice fringes, and the lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, shown in Fig. 1 (c (i)(inset)), corresponds to the inner (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon, indicating excellent crystallinity, graphitic structure, and satisfactory graphitization. The outer shell's poor graphitization can be ascribed to the high abundance of nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups present at the carbon edges. There have been reports of CDs exhibiting similar characteristics synthesized from green materials (Ferjani et al., 2024). The TEM image of the SCNFs reveals a uniform and homogeneous fiber morphology, devoid of amorphous lignin and hemicellulose polymeric domains, which exhibit a tendency for aggregation. The fiber diameter, measured using ImageJ software, ranges from 40 to 95 nm, as shown in Fig. 1 (c (iii)(inset)). Fig. 1 (c (iv)) demonstrated that CDs were conventionally embedded into the SCNFs matrix, ensuring their efficient distribution for the maximum available functionalized surface. We observed that incorporating CDs into the nanofiber matrix doesn't significantly change the fiber diameter because the dots are well-distributed within the fibers. However, the subsequent silanization step does lead to a very slight increase in diameter. This minor change is due to the formation of a thin, uniform silane layer on the fiber surfaces rather than any swelling or fusion of the fibers. Basically, the core structure of the nanofibers remained intact, while the surface modification offered additional functionality without compromising the overall morphology (Taib et al., 2024). Moreover, the size of CDs was slightly increased from about ≈3.5 nm to ≈4.8 nm in SCNFs@CDs due to the interaction and incorporation of CDs into the SCNFs matrix as shown in Fig. 1 (c (iv)(inset)). This increase can be related to interaction of SCNFs matrix with CDs functional groups, aggregation phenomena, surface functionalization, or encapsulation of CDs into the SCNFs matrix. Similar observations were also reported by other researchers (Ahn et al., 2021; Junka et al., 2014).

The XPS studies were performed to analyze the surface states and chemical composition of synthesized materials. The XPS survey spectra of CNFs and SCNFs (Fig. 2 ) indicated the presence of C 1s (285.5eV), N 1s (400.4eV), O 1s (532.2eV), and Si 2p (102.1eV), respectively. The appearance of the N 1s and Si 2p peaks in the XPS survey spectra of SCNFs proved that APTES has been successfully grafted onto the surface of the cellulose to produce SCNFs as shown in Fig. 2 (n). The silicon concentration scaled from 0 % to 3.12 %, whereas the nitrogen level improved from 0 % to 1.82 % after modification with APTES. Fig. 2 (a) and (f), respectively, depict the deconvolution of the C1s peak of CNFs and SCNFs. In the C 1s spectra of CNFs, peaks at 283.9 eV and 286.4 eV, as illustrated in Fig. 2 (a), are attributed to C-C/C-H and C-O bonds (Kaur et al., 2021). Conversely, C1s spectra of SCNFs (Fig. 2 (f)) exhibit three peaks at 284.3 eV, 286.1 eV, and 287.8 eV, corresponding to C-H/C-C, C-N, and O-C-O/C=O bonds, respectively. The emergence of a new peak at 287.8 eV may be recognized in the C1s spectra of SCNF which demonstrates the binding interactions between APTES and CNFs. Comparing Fig. 2 (b) and (g), a shift of 0.20 eV corresponds to the C=O and C-O bonds along with a new peak at 534 eV corresponding to Si-O bond, supports the modification of cellulose by APTES through binding to alcoholic groups of CNFs. Fig. 2 (b) showed two peaks at binding energy 531.26 eV and 532.9 eV, corresponding to the C-O and C=O bond in CNFs, whereas Fig. 2 (g) shows three peaks at 531.04 eV, 532.3 eV, and 534 eV, corresponding to the newly formed bonds C-O, C=O, and Si-O in SNCFs, respectively. The shift in the proportion of the O 1s component demonstrated the existence of a chemical connection between APTES and CNFs. The N 1s spectra of SCNFs, (Fig. 2 (h)), exhibits two additional peaks at 396.6 eV; 401.5 eV, which corresponds to the N-H and C-N bonds in amine group of SCNFs, thereby further confirming the transformation of CNFs into SCNFs. Peaks at 101.9 and 104.5 in the Si 2p spectra of SCNFs, shown in Fig. 2 (l), which corresponds to Si-O-Si and Si-C/Si-O respectively (Tian et al., 2022). Fig. 2 (n) depicts the complete scan XPS spectrum of the CDs, exhibits three peaks at 532.13eV, 400.5eV, and 285.4eV, which correspond to O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s, respectively. Deconvolution of the C 1s spectra of CDs (Fig. 2 (c)) reveals the existence of three peaks at 284.7, 285.5, and 287.8 eV corresponding to the C-C/C-H, C-N, and C=O groups. Additionally, two peaks at 531.7 and 533.2 eV were visible in the high-resolution O 1s spectra (Fig. 2 (d)), which were caused by the existence of C=O and COOH groups, respectively. Two peaks at 399.4 eV and 399.7 eV in the nitrogen spectra (Fig. 2 (e)) were attributed to the presence of C-N and N-H bonds, respectively (Ferjani et al., 2024). The XPS survey spectra of SCNFs@CDs (Fig. 2 (n)) indicated the presence of C 1s (285.5eV), O 1s (532.2eV), N 1s (400.4eV), and Si 2p (102.1eV), respectively. Peaks at 284.5 eV, 286.1 eV, and 287.1 eV due to C-C/C-H, C-N, and C=O groups, respectively can be seen after deconvolution of the C 1s spectra of SCNFs@CDs (Fig. 2 (i)). Further, a shift in peak-position in the high-resolution C 1s spectra of SCNFs@CDs confirmed the binding interaction between SCNFs and CDs, whereas Fig. 2 (j) shows three peaks at 531.1 eV, 532.2 eV, and 534.1 eV, corresponding to the bonds C-O, C=O, and Si-O in SCNFs@CDs, respectively. Peaks at 101.9 eV and 102.9 eV in the Si 2p spectra of SCNFs@CDs, shown in Fig. 2 (m), correspond to Si-O-Si and Si-C/Si-O, respectively. The high-resolution N 1s spectra (Fig. 2 (k)) of SCNFs@CDs also provide significant proof for the interactions of amino groups on SCNFs and carboxyl groups on CDs. The shifting in N-H peak of the N 1s component was also observed. This observation may be attributed to the formation of covalent bonds via amide linkage between the amino groups on SCNFs and carboxyl groups on CDs. This binding interaction further confirms the successful functionalization and is consistent with the FTIR results.

The morphological structures of synthesized materials were investigated using FESEM. The surface morphology of pristine samples and after surface modification of various samples is apparent in FESEM micrographs of CNFs, SCNFs, and SCNFs@CDs, as shown in Fig. 3
. As compared to the CNFs (Fig. 3 (a and b)), which are distinguishable fibers (Kaur et al., 2021), SCNFs (Fig. 3 (c and d)) appears to be a porous network of fibrous material. SCNFs showed a considerable increase in porosity due to the formation of a huge Si-O-C porous network on their surface (Komal et al., 2019). The surface morphology of SCNFs@CDs (Fig. 3 (e and f)) demonstrates that CDs are homogeneously dispersed on the surface of SCNFs. CNFs provide a smoother, more ordered, and more homogeneous surface as compared to SCNFs. Furthermore, the embedding of CDs on the surface of SCNFs increased the roughness of SCNFs@CDs composite. The network is proposed to facilitate more rapid penetration of analyte molecules into the inner space and provide an abundant supply of ion coordinating sites beneficial for detection and adsorption. Fig. S3 (i, and ii) show EDX and mapping images of SCNFs and SCNFs@CDs. Mapping images of SCNFs (Fig. S3 (i)) show solely carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, forming the framework of SCNFs. The identification of nitrogen in all grafted fibers, alongside carbon and oxygen, suggests substantial grafting of amino groups onto the CNF surface. The increase in nitrogen content in SCNFs@CDs (Fig. S3 (ii)) is notable, resulting from covalent bonding via the formation of an amide linkage between the amino groups on SCNFs and the carboxyl groups on CDs. Furthermore, the functionalization is consistent throughout the entire surface of the SCNFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interactions between CDs and SCNFs matrix in the composite
ce:section.ce:para: The literature indicate that if there is just physical adsorption between CDs and SCNFs matrix in the composite, the absorption spectrum similar to that CDs will ensue (Sun et al., 2014). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, SCNFs, and SCNFs@CDs were recorded in the range of 250–500 nm (Fig. 4(a)). When comparing the spectra of SCNFs@CDs to that of SCNFs, an additional wide band in the range of 280–380 nm was also noticed, which can probably be due to the absorption of CDs. However, the absorption spectrum of CDs in water was undoubtedly distinct from the above results, suggesting that SCNFs and CDs have a robust connection, encompassing more than simple physical absorption. The difference in spectra of CDs and SCNFs@CDs corroborates the hypothesis that a strong chemical interaction exists between SCNFs and CDs. Fig. 4 (b) depicts the emission spectra of CDs, SCNFs and SCNFs@CDs when excited at 360 nm. The SCNFs@CDs showed a wide peak at around 445 nm, which is ascribed to the presence of CDs. Specifically, the emission intensity of the SCNFs@CDs improved significantly after the addition of CDs (QY ≈ 70 %) to the SCNFs matrix. This observation further substantiates that SCNFs may have interacted chemically with CDs (You et al., 2016). Additionally, the fluorescence intensity variations and emission characteristics of SCNFs@CDs at different excitation wavelengths in the range of 300 nm–400 nm were explored. As observed in Fig. 4 (c), the FL emission of SCNFs@CDs initially increased with a maximum FL intensity at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, while a subsequent decrease in FL intensity was observed, thereafter. Further, as the excitation wavelength shifted from 300 nm to 400 nm, the emission wavelength of SCNFs@CDs shifted from 445 nm to 480 nm. This demonstrated the excitation-dependent performance of SCNFs@CDs. The results further confirm a strong interaction between CDs and SCNFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of SCNFs@CDs for detection of UO2 2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: For practical sensing applications of SCNFs@CDs, its fluorescence stability under various chemical conditions is essential. Therefore, the fluorescence behavior of SCNFs@CDs samples at different pH levels (pH 1 to 12) and at different time intervals (up to 3 months) was examined. Fig. 4 (d) depicts the effect of solution pH on the FL response of SCNFs@CDs. The FL response of SCNFs@CDs was observed to be highest under neutral conditions, with a sharp decrease in both highly acidic and alkaline conditions. This sharp change is most likely due to protonation, deprotonation, or degradation of surface functional groups (Zou et al., 2020). To assess the FL stability of SCNFs@CDs over time, the composite was stored under ambient conditions and its optical response was recorded over a three-month period. The results showed adequate fluorescence stability of the composite, with a decrease of only 2.05 % after 3 months, as depicted in Fig. 4 (e). Next, we investigated the influence of various solvents on the FL response of the sensor to achieve deeper insight into its sensitivity and performance. The FL spectra of SCNFs@CDs were measured in a range of polar protic, polar aprotic, and non-polar solvents, such as water, mesitylene, dimethylformamide, methanol, toluene, benzene, DMSO, ethanol. The FL response is strongly influenced by the solvent's dipole moment, dielectric constant, and hydrogen-bonding capacity with the surface functional groups of the nanocomposite (Mukherjee et al., 2017; Pawar et al., 2019). Among all solvents, the FL response demonstrated optimal results in water due to its high dielectric constant and super hydrogen bonding ability with surface function groups of SCNFs@CDs. These interactions contribute to the stabilization of the excited electronic states and the reduction of non-radiative decay pathways, thereby improving the fluorescence signal in comparison to other solvents (Fig. 4 (f)), leading to its selection for all further investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of SCNFs@CDs for detection of UO2 2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: For selectivity studies, FL response of SCNFs@CDs was tested for different metal ions including As3+, Gd3+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Au3+, Co2+, and UO2 2+ at same concentration (100 mg L−1, pH 7). As illustrated in (Fig. 5 (a)), when As3+, Gd3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Au3+, and Co2+, metal ions were introduced into the SCNFs@CDs aqueous solution, the FL intensity of SCNFs@CDs was slightly quenched. However, the presence of Cd2+ and Fe3+ ions led to a more noticeable decrease in FL intensity, dropping it to 30.4 % and 36.3 %, respectively. The most significant effect, however, was seen with UO2 2+ ions, which caused the FL intensity to decrease drastically to approx. 77.7 %. The strong selectivity of SCNFs@CDs towards UO2 2+ ions is due to its strong chelation with nitrogen and oxygen donor groups, influenced by uranium's higher coordination ability and HSAB principles (Beer et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2023). These findings demonstrated that the SCNFs@CDs exhibited remarkable sensitivity towards UO2 2+ ions. Further, FL response of SCNFs@CDs was recorded for UO2 2+ ions in presence of possible interferent metals ions. It was observed that the FL response of SCNFs@CDs barely altered due to the existence of other interfering ions, indicating that the nanocomposite exhibits a high level of selectivity for UO2 2+ ions and that coexisting ions have minimal effect on the FL intensity (Fig. 5 (b)). To analyze the response time of SCNFs@CDs towards UO2 2+ ions, the influence of interaction time between SCNFs@CDs and UO2 2+ on fluorescence intensity was also explored. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 (c), the FL response of the SCNFs@CDs reduced sharply upon adding of UO2 2+ ions and stabilized after 3 min, demonstrating that SCNFs@CDs interacted with UO2 2+ ions very quickly thus giving a rapid response as the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response curve of SCNFs@CDs for detection of UO2 2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: By monitoring the FL emission intensity under excitation at 360 nm for different UO2 2+ ion concentrations, the quenching degree of UO2 2+ ions on the FL intensity of SCNFs@CDs was studied. Fig. 5 (d) illustrates that the FL emission of SCNFs@CDs decreases continuously as the UO2 2+ ions concentration increases. The fluorescence quenching intensity of SCNFs@CDs exhibited a linear correlation with the concentration of UO2 2+ ions, ranging from 0.02 to 70 μM.

Based on the detection limit calculations using the equation, limit of detection (LOD) = 3σ/k (where σ denotes the standard deviation of the signal (n = 3), and k indicates the slope of the curve between fluorescence intensity and metal ions concentration), the LOD for UO2 2+ using SCNFs@CDs was determined to be as low as 1.64 nM. This is significantly lower than the existing maximum limit for uranium (15 ppb) of UO2 2+ in drinking water established by WHO (Sahoo et al., 2020). The linear correlation coefficient is R2 = 0.998 (Fig. 5 (e)), and the linear regression equation is F0/F = 0.05735C-1.06436. The comparison between various CDs composite-based fluorescent materials and techniques reported for the quantification of heavy metals has been detailed in Table S2, in the supplementary data. Overall, these results highlight that SCNFs@CDs composite is a promising probe for fluorescence-based monitoring of UO2 2+ ions due to its highly sensitivity and selectivity towards UO2 2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UO2 2+detection in spiked lake water using SCNFs@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the reliability and accuracy of the proposed fluorescence sensor, UO2 2+ ions were detected in real samples taken from Sukhna lake, Chandigarh using a standard addition approach. Table 1 depicts the outcomes of the real water studies. The spike recovery of this approach was determined by adding various concentrations of UO2 2+ ions standard solution to the samples. Satisfactory recoveries (98 %–101 %) with a low relative standard deviation were achieved, demonstrating that SCNFs@CDs fluorescent composites have a significant potential for the quantification of UO2 2+ ions in real matrix like lake water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption experiments of UO2 2+ ion onto SCNFs@CDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Apart from sensing characteristics, SCNFs@CDs composite was also examined for the adsorption capacity of hazardous UO2 2+ ions. Role of several parameters such as solution pH, SCNFs@CDs dosage, and concentration of UO2 2+ ions on the adsorption process was investigated to understand of the adsorption phenomenon of UO2 2+ ions on SCNFs@CDs and for optimal performance of the nanocomposite. To ascertain the adsorption mechanism, various adsorption kinetic models and adsorption isotherms were fitted to the experimental data.

Solution pH plays a crucial role in the adsorption of metal ions on an adsorbent as protonation and deprotonation of functional groups happen concerning changes in the solution pH. Considering this, the ZPC experiments were performed to analyze the surface charge of nanocomposite SCNFs@CDs (Section S6 of supplementary file). ZPC is the pH where there is absolutely no charge density on the adsorbent's surface. The ZPC for the composite SCNFs@CDs was assessed as pH 5.6, demonstrating that the overall charge of the nanocomposite was zero at this pH (Fig. 6 (a) ). The surface charge of SCNFs@CDs would be negative above pH 5.6 and positive in the solution below pH 5.6. After determining the ZPC of SCNFs@CDs, the influence of solution pH (pH 1–8) on the removal efficiency of UO2 2+ (Initial concentration: 100 mg L−1) was investigated using SCNFs@CDs. As depicted in Fig. 6 (b), a 60.91 % removal efficiency for UO2 2+ ions were achieved at pH 2, which increased to 93.37 % at pH 7. In the acidic medium (pH ≤ 4), U(VI) primarily exists as UO2 2+, (UO2)4(OH)7 +, and UO2OH+, while the surface charge of the nanocomposite remains positive, leading to reduced adsorption. However, around pH 7, occurrence of (UO2)2(OH)4 2− and UO2(OH)2 0 forms start dominating with a tepid presence of UO2OH+ and (UO2)2(OH)+ which strongly interact with the negatively charged composite surface, enhancing adsorption. At pH 8.0, the removal efficiency was further observed to reduce to 81.5 %, likely due to the conversion of UO2 2+ ions into dominant hydrolyzed species including UO2(CO3)2 2−, UO2(CO3)4-, (UO2)(CO3)(OH)3 4−, (UO2)3(OH)10 4− and UO2(CO3)3 4− which may exhibit weaker interactions with the composite surface (Xin et al., 2024). Consequently, all adsorption experiments for UO2 2+ ions were performed at pH 7.

Further, the adsorption performance of the nanocomposite was tested at various contact time intervals ranging from 0 to 35 min. The adsorption graphs (Fig. 6 (c)) clearly show rapid adsorption. The rapid rate of UO2 2+ adsorption observed during the first 5 min after the addition of SCNFs@CDs suggests that UO2 2+ ions are surface complexed with SCNFs@CDs or have undergone strong chemisorption (Wang et al., 2025). A consistent equilibrium adsorption capacity of 93.37 % for SCNFs@CDs led to the equilibrium state being reached after 35 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of initial concentrations of adsorbate and adsorbent
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 (d) demonstrates the influence of initial concentration of UO2 2+ on adsorption capacity. When the initial concentration of UO2 2+ varied from 50 to 300 mg L−1, the adsorption of ions on SCNFs@CDs increased. An increase in adsorbate concentration in the solution leads to greater involvement of active sites present on the surface of SCNFs@CDs in the adsorption process, thereby enhancing the driving force of the solid-liquid phase to overcome mass transfer resistance overall (Rani et al., 2024). Improved adsorption capacity is achieved due to increased interaction between UO2 2+ and active sites on the adsorbent (Wang et al., 2025).

In addition to adsorbate concentration, the adsorbent dosage is a key parameter in avoiding the consumption of excessive adsorbent. As we increase the concentration of adsorbent, the available surface area expands, providing a wider range of potentially active sites for adsorption (Rani et al., 2024). The influence of SCNFs@CDs nanocomposite dosage on the adsorption of UO2 2+ ions was investigated using adsorbent amounts ranging from 20 to 100 mg per 100 mL solution at a fixed concentration of UO2 2+ ions (100 mg L−1 and pH 7). As illustrated in Fig. 6 (e), increasing the SCNFs@CDs dosage at a fixed concentration of UO2 2+ ions boosted UO2 2+ ions adsorption from 80.01 % to 93.37 %.

For the practical application of removing UO2 2+ ions using an adsorbent, it is crucial to have an in-depth understanding of adsorption kinetics, as this allows for insights into rate determining step and the adsorption mechanism. Therefore, adsorption of UO2 2+ ions on SCNFs@CDs was investigated using PFO, and PSO kinetic models. The best-fitted kinetic model was identified by thoroughly analyzing and evaluating experimental data over varying periods and different adsorbent and adsorbate dosages. The linear PFO and PSO kinetics of UO2 2+ ions were explored in a wide concentration range of 50–300 mg L−1 to ensure the rapidity and efficacy of SCNFs@CDs to remove UO2 2+ ions. Fig. 7 ( a and b) depicts a linearized form of the PFO and PSO kinetics models for UO2 2+ ions, meanwhile, Table 2 presents the kinetic parameters for both models. As per the results depicted in Table 2, the PSO kinetic model best describes UO2 2+ ions adsorption by SCNFs@CDs with higher correlation coefficients (R2) than the PFO models. Thus, it is anticipated that UO2 2+ ions are primarily adsorbed onto the surface of SCNFs@CDs through the formation of chemical bonds with its surface functional groups, indicating that the process is predominantly chemisorption (Kaur et al., 2021).

An adsorption isotherm describes the correlation of amount of an adsorbate on the adsorbent's surface and its concentration in the solution at equilibrium (Wang et al., 2025). Consequently, adsorption data of UO2 2+ ions on SCNFs@CDs was fitted using Freundlich, and Langmuir isotherm models (equations are given in Table S1 in supplementary file), to investigate the possible interactions between ions and nanocomposite. Fig. 7 (c and d) demonstrates the linear experimental fit for both isotherms' models, and Table 3 displays the isotherm parameters.

Results in Table 3 suggests that Langmuir isotherm effectively characterizes UO2 2+ ions adsorption on SCNFs@CDs, as indicated by the higher correlation coefficient value (R2 = 0.9910) and maximum adsorption capacity (196.07 mg g−1) compared to the other model employed. These results are comparable to the adsorption capacities of other reported adsorbent materials for UO2 2+ ions (Table S3 in supplementary file).

Since the Langmuir model assumes a homogeneous monolayer adsorption process, the adsorption of UO2 2+ ions onto the uniform surface of SCNFs@CDs follows a monolayer adsorption mechanism The Langmuir constant (KL) of 0.0705 m3g-1 indicates a significant interaction between SCNFs@CDs and UO2 2+ ions (Wang et al., 2025).

Further, the separation factor (RL) was determined using the following equation (2). Where, Co (mg L−1) and KL, denote UO2 2+ concentration in the solution and the Langmuir constant, respectively.
(2)

There are four conceivable cases: RL = 0 denotes irreversible adsorption, 0< RL < 1 shows favorable adsorption, RL = 1 suggests linear adsorption, and RL > 1 indicates unfavorable adsorption.

For the initial UO2 2+ ions concentration, which ranged from 50 mg L−1 to 300 mg L−1 resulted in calculated RL value for SCNFs@CDs varied between 0.2209 and 0.045. These findings indicate favorable UO2 2+ ions adsorption, with values of 0< RL < 1. Freundlich isotherm is used to characterize adsorption on multilayers and heterogeneous surfaces, accounting for various special interactions such as van der Waals forces and electrostatics. Fig. 7 (d) and Table 3 shows linearized Freundlich isotherm plots, with computed values of n and KF of 2.75 and 33.89, respectively. The 1/n value determined by Freundlich isotherm for SCNFs@CDs was 0.3637. The variation of 1/n from 0 to 1 indicates that UO2 2+ ions adsorption on SCNFs@CDs was favorable (Kaur et al., 2021).

To assess the reusability of the prepared nanocomposite, adsorption-desorption cycles were systematically performed using HCl at varying concentrations ranging from 0.001 M to 0.1 Among these, 0.1 M HCl was found to be most effective in desorbing UO2 2+ ions from SCNFs@CDs surface, making it the optimal choice for adsorption-desorption studies. Adsorption-desorption behavior of SCNFs@CDs nanocomposite was tested for 5 consecutive cycles (Fig. 8 ). The results showed that the % adsorption efficiency of the composite remained high even after 5 cycles of adsorption/desorption, with only a slight decrease from 93.37 % to 87.32 % confirming the reusability of the adsorbent. The observed decrease in efficiency is likely due to the partial damage or deactivation of active sites upon repeated exposure to the acidic desorption environment (Liu et al., 2024b). HCl effectively removes adsorbed UO2 2+ ions, but it may also affect the chemical stability of the functional groups responsible for adsorption, leading to a gradual reduction in available active sites and may affect the overall adsorption capacity. Nevertheless, the nanocomposite retains a significant portion of its adsorption capability, highlighting its potential for multiple reuse cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between UO2 2+ ions and SCNFs@CDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy was utilized to investigate possible interactions between UO2 2+ ions and SCNFs@CDs. The FL decays of the SCNFs@CDs at the emission maximum were initially seen irrespective of the presence or absence UO2 2+ ions (Fig. 9 (a) ). Adding UO2 2+ ions increased the fluorescence lifetime of SCNFs@CDs from 3.78 ns to 4.55 ns, indicating a charge transfer between UO2 2+ ions and SCNFs@CDs (Wang et al., 2022). Furthermore, the mechanism of binding of UO2 2+ ions with SCNFs@CDs can be supported based on hard-soft acid base principle (HSAB) and hydrogen bonding as shown in Fig. 9 (b). In this complexation, the hard acids i.e. oxygen and nitrogen groups of SCNFs@CDs attract UO2 2+ ion through HSAB interaction. In addition to this, there is hydrogen bonding present between the amide group and the electronegative oxygen present in UO2 2+ ion resulting in both the sensing and adsorption of UO2 2+ ion (Beer et al., 2010).

Further, to study the interaction between SCNFs@CDs and UO2 2+ ions and to investigate the structural modifications before and after adsorption of UO2 2+ ions on SNCFs@CDs, XPS was used. The existence of UO2 2+ ions in the full range of XPS spectra is demonstrated by the new peaks at about 400 eV in the high resolution of the U 4f spectrum (Fig. 10 (j) ). It further reveals that the UO2 2+ ions were adsorbed on the surfaces of SCNFs@CDs composites. Two additional peaks of the binding energies 381.7 eV and 392.5 eV are assigned to U 4f7/2 and U 4f5/2. Fig. 10 (b)) shows the N 1s spectrum, with peaks for C-N and N-H at 399.2 eV and 400.8 eV, respectively (Wang et al., 2022). When SCNFs@CDs composites were combined with UO2 2+ ions, the two peaks shifted to a higher binding energy (Fig. 10 (d)). This indicates that the amide from SCNFs@CDs was strongly linked with UO2 2+ ions. Next in the O 1s spectrum given in Fig. 10 (e), three peak at 531.3 eV, 532.7 eV, 534.2 eV were observed corresponding to C-O, C=O, and Si-O respectively. The three O 1s peaks of SCNFs@CDs combined with UO2 2+ ions significantly moved to higher binding energies, and the intensity decreased significantly. Further, this demonstrates that the carboxyl groups in SCNFs@CDs have strongly interacted with UO2 2+ ions. The XPS analysis results of SCNFs@CDs before and after adsorption demonstrated that the binding of UO2 2+ ions to the SCNFs@CDs is predominantly a chemical process.

In summary, a dual-functional fluorescent nanocomposite (SCNFs@CDs) utilizing Kalanchoe pinnata as a sustainable carbon precursor was developed for environmental remediation. The remarkable performance of SCNFs@CDs as both a fluorescent probe and an adsorbent for hazardous UO2 2+ ions underscore their potential for addressing critical environmental challenges. The achieved limit of detection (LOD) of 1.64 nM, coupled with exceptional selectivity in the presence of competing ions, positions SCNFs@CDs as a superior option for uranium sensing and removal.

Furthermore, the demonstrated maximum adsorption capacity of 196.07 mg g−1, along with high reusability, highlights their practicality for real-world applications. The adherence to pseudo-second-order kinetics and the Langmuir isotherm model confirms the effectiveness and stability of the adsorption process. This innovative approach not only provides a viable solution for the detection and removal of UO2 2+ ions but also promotes the use of eco-friendly materials in water treatment technologies. As we continue to confront environmental contamination issues, SCNFs@CDs emerge as a promising candidate for enhancing water quality and safeguarding public health.
